senses.gloss: Homs (a city, the capital of the governorate of Homs, in western Syria)

examples.text: 1267, Abu al-Baqa al-Rundi, Elegy for al-Andalus

etymology.text:
Compare Akkadian 𒈝𒈬𒌅 (ḫummuṭu, “to apply heat, to burn, to scorch, to parch, to roast”).

The vowel variation points to it being borrowed from Aramaic חִימְצָא (ḥimməṣā) / חוּמְצָא (ḥumməṣā, “chickpea”). Cognate with Hebrew חִמְצָה (ḥimṣā́), possibly also related to Akkadian 𒋰 (TAB /⁠ḫamāṭu⁠/, “to apply heat, to burn, to scorch, to parch, to roast”), in the sense of being dehydrated or dried out beans.

From جُنْد حِمْص, “the military district or legion of Ḥims”, from Ancient Greek Ἔμεσᾰ (Émesă), descendant of Aramaic חֲמָת (ḥamāth, “fortress”, literally “frustration, hindrance”) + צובָא (ṣōwḇā, “near, surrounding, station”).

it is white, and red, and black, and of a sort called

and is also wild, and cultivated in gardens: the wild sort is the hotter, and the more contracted; the nutriment of the garden-sort is the better; and the black is the most powerful in its operations:
it is flatulent, lenitive, diuretic, having the property of increasing the seminal fluid and the carnal appetite and the blood:
it has in it two substances, which quit it by cooking; one of them salt,

saline, which is lenitive; and the other sweet, which is diuretic; and it clears away spots in the skin, and beautifies the complexion, and is beneficial for hot tumours, and its oil is serviceable for the ringworm,
tetter; and its meal, for the fluid of foul ulcers; and the infusion thereof, for toothache, and for swelling of the lip; and it clears the voice:
it also strengthens the body and the penis;
wherefore it is given as fodder to the stallions of horses and the like, and of camels;
on the condition of its being eaten not before
food nor after it, but in the midst thereof;
(K;) or, correctly, as in the Minháj,
it should be eaten between two meals.
